cot_content:
Okay, so the question is about how to tell your right and left earbuds apart without looking. The options are A and B.

Option A says to tie a small knot at the speaker end of the left one. Option B says to tie a small knot at the connecting end of the left one.

Hmm, I need to think about how earbuds are structured. Earbuds typically have two parts: the speaker part that goes into the ear and the connecting wire that links to the other earbud and the device. So, the "speaker end" would be the part you insert into your ear, and the "connecting end" would be the part that's attached to the cable.

If you tie a knot at the speaker end (option A), when you put them in your ears, the knot would be near your ear. This might help you identify which one is the left one by touch. But if you tie a knot at the connecting end (option B), that would be on the part of the earbud that's attached to the wire. Since the connecting end is part of the wire, it might be harder to feel when you're wearing them.

I've heard that people often use small knots or other markers on the speaker end to distinguish left and right. That way, when you pop them in, you can feel the knot on one side and know which ear it goes into. The connecting end is usually part of the wire that's not in direct contact with your ear, so a knot there might not be as noticeable.

So, based on this reasoning, option A (tying a knot at the speaker end) makes more sense for identifying the left earbud by touch without looking.
label: majority
